133369.explanation: The VVD is not in favor of a referendum. In our democracy, residents are allowed to choose their municipal council members once every four years. Based on this political mandate, the municipal council sometimes has to make difficult choices and weigh different interests.

133370.explanation: The VVD does not favor this. The municipality has several subsidy frameworks in which this is already possible, no separate framework needs to be added. Furthermore, it is the municipality’s task to make policies on green and environment; residents can contact their local council members about this.

133371.explanation: The VVD stands for sound financial policy. We have not raised property tax (OZB) in recent years.

133372.explanation: Under VVD leadership, the dog tax in our municipality has been abolished.

133373.explanation: VVD Haarlemmermeer chooses innovative and efficient maintenance with modern techniques, respecting nature and animals. Depending on the case, it can differ whether agricultural land can or cannot be converted into a nature reserve.

133374.explanation: The VVD encourages residents to generate sustainable energy but does not need to make a financial contribution to this.

133375.explanation: We provide no assistance to people without legal residence rights – enforcement is clear and consistent. There is no place for illegal immigrants in Haarlemmermeer.

133376.explanation: The VVD does not support giving priority for housing to asylum seekers. In Haarlemmermeer, this has already been abolished under the VVD.

133378.explanation: We create space for innovation, growth, and sustainability. The biggest obstacle to growth is the lack of space: in the power grid, on business parks, and in regulations. By being innovative with our space, we create more opportunities for our entrepreneurs.

133379.explanation: The VVD supports the construction of solar parks where this is possible.

133380.explanation: There are already schemes in place that do this. We will maintain these but see no reason to expand them.

133381.explanation: The VVD values Haarlemmermeer's culture. Using the Cruquius Museum as an example, we support local cultural entrepreneurship.

133382.explanation: The VVD supports investing in safety and therefore also investments in BOA's (public enforcement officers) who focus on traffic safety.

133383.explanation: As VVD, we want to make significant investments in safety in the coming years. Our municipality is growing, and so are the problems. Camera surveillance where necessary is an important part of this.

133384.explanation: As VVD, we want to make significant investments in safety in the coming years. Our municipality is growing, and so are the problems. Traffic safety is an important part of this.

133385.explanation: As VVD, we want to make significant investments in safety in the coming years. Our municipality is growing, and so are the problems. Good equipment for BOA's is an important part of this.

133386.explanation: The current starter loan helps first-time home buyers. We must retain this for Haarlemmermeer residents purchasing a home for the first time.

133387.explanation: Housing solutions used for asylum seekers must also be used at least 50% for our own residents.

133388.explanation: The VVD focuses on affordable construction. This means not only social rental housing but also affordable owner-occupied homes. This way, we ensure turnover on the housing market.

133389.explanation: The VVD wants to continue focusing on building homes in the coming years. In some centers this means building within the existing core; in others by adding new neighborhoods.

133390.explanation: As VVD, we see participation in society as an important foundation for creating self-worth. An active contribution to our society should be natural, even if you cannot work. Those unwilling to participate can no longer count on a permanent safety net from the municipality.

133392.explanation: In the areas where traffic safety is at risk, we will implement traffic measures. We do not support a general 30 km/h speed limit in all centers, including where it is not needed.

133393.explanation: For the VVD, it is not the municipality's role to express opinions on international conflicts. This is the task of the national government and Parliament. We believe municipal councillors should not be forced to take a stand on these issues.

133394.explanation: Haarlemmermeer has grown significantly in recent years. As VVD, we want to continue this growth. Necessarily, the municipality must grow as well, but we will limit this to what is truly needed. For example, in safety (police and enforcement), the municipality must grow accordingly.

133395.explanation: The VVD wants Schiphol to be able to grow sustainably in the future. This is not only good for our economy but also for employment.

133396.explanation: Schiphol must be able to grow sustainably. The VVD supports a night closure of Schiphol to reduce noise nuisance. This must be done in consultation with all stakeholders. The municipality should not create obstacles but seek the best solution for our residents and the economy.

133397.explanation: The VVD believes that entrepreneurs in Haarlemmermeer should be able to continue their businesses in our municipality. This means no extra rules beyond those determined nationally. This way, we keep Haarlemmermeer a pleasant place to do business.

133398.explanation: The VVD is against any increase of the property tax (OZB), in any form.

133399.explanation: The VVD will always stand up for equality and combating discrimination. We have ensured that the municipality pays attention to this through various campaigns. We will maintain these but do not see a need to expand them.

133400.explanation: Wind turbines are only an option for the VVD if there is more than sufficient support among residents. The VVD is not in favor of placing more wind turbines than currently agreed upon with resident support.

133401.explanation: The VVD wants to specifically connect practically trained jobs with education and work. Due to labor shortages, we will need everyone. By strengthening the connection between education and work, companies and schools can arrange more internships themselves.

133402.explanation: Membership in Haarlemmermeer is free up to 26 years old. To encourage young people to read, we will maintain this. For adults, the VVD supports a personal contribution for a subscription.

133403.explanation: In Haarlemmermeer, we promote top talent. Our athletes must have the chance to compete internationally. For this, we have TopsUPport Haarlemmermeer, aimed at athletes up to 30 years old living in Haarlemmermeer.

133404.explanation: VVD Haarlemmermeer stands for innovative cultural policy. This includes large events like Mysteryland. We will continue to support these initiatives that put our municipality on the map.

133406.explanation: The VVD wants to significantly invest in additional resources for safety in the coming years. Part of this includes allocating more funds to combat (undermining) crime.

133407.explanation: The VVD wants to significantly invest in safety in the coming years. Part of this is the Neighborhood BOA, where each neighborhood/core gets its own BOA.

133408.explanation: The VVD opposes unnecessary building regulations that prevent investment in housing and the growth of our municipality. However, we want to keep building with Haarlemmermeer quality. This way, our villages and neighborhoods remain beautiful in the future. Preferably through agreements rather than rules.

133409.explanation: As VVD, we want to continue investing heavily in housing construction in the coming years. In some villages, this means mainly building within existing village boundaries; in others, it means building a new neighborhood.

133410.explanation: The VVD wants to prevent the social safety net from becoming a hammock. This means that extra-minimum social provisions must be curtailed. In Haarlemmermeer, we currently do more than legally required. Where possible, we want to abolish these extra rules.

133411.explanation: A social assistance benefit should be properly assessed to prevent abuse. The VVD is committed to ensuring that everyone receives this promptly but does not support setting a time limit if this compromises the quality of the assessment.

133412.explanation: The VVD is against reintroducing the Haarlemmermeer Pass. Past experience has shown it is not used well and often does not reach the residents who need it most.

133413.explanation: It is important that every child can have breakfast to fully participate at school. The municipality contributes to this through existing initiatives. We cannot do this for all schools. Via the national program, the national government is responsible for Breakfast at School, not the municipality.

133414.explanation: The VVD is and remains opposed to paid parking. For us, paid parking is only a last option and may only be introduced with ample support from the local community. For example, in the Hoofddorp shopping center, paid parking is necessary due to congestion.

133415.explanation: In Haarlemmermeer, you should be able to travel by your own choice. We invest in good accessibility for cyclists and cars. We ensure that cycle paths do not come at the expense of other road users.

133416.explanation: As VVD, we consider a healthy living environment important. In the coming years, we will continue to ensure that Haarlemmermeer remains pleasant to live in. We invest in green spaces where needed but do not support a general promise to plant two trees for every one cut down.

133417.explanation: The VVD wants to invest heavily in safety in the coming years. Traffic safety is also part of this. This is especially important for young road users. Through existing initiatives, we support improving traffic safety for young people.

133418.explanation: We assist shopkeepers by making clear agreements about parking policy. By making smart agreements, we can use parking garages of offices and companies in the city center on shopping evenings and weekends. The VVD prefers agreements over compensation.

133419.explanation: The VVD firmly supports the current generous parking standards in our municipality. This ensures that you can get around quickly and comfortably. Through smart solutions, we don’t have to replace parking spaces with green areas but can combine parking with a pleasant living environment.

133420.explanation: Informal caregivers are an indispensable link within our public health. Their presence and involvement improve care and keep it affordable. Therefore, we focus on low-threshold support to prevent overload within existing national government arrangements.

133421.explanation: Controlling the costs of youth care is a shared responsibility. Therefore, we seek cooperation with general practitioners and emphasize parental responsibility. Not all issues belong to the municipality. This way, we ensure good care in the future.

133422.explanation: The VVD first focuses on low-threshold support and a strong front-line through prevention via schools, sports clubs, general practitioners, and neighborhood teams like Maatvast. Not all problems fall under the municipality’s responsibility. We also stress the responsibility of parents in upbringing.

133423.explanation: The VVD wants Haarlemmermeer to remain a pleasant place to live in the future. To grow as a municipality, we must also ensure sufficient space for recreation and greenery. PARK21 is an important part of this. The VVD remains vigilant about the development costs.

133424.explanation: The VVD wants every resident of Haarlemmermeer to feel free and safe. Unfortunately, this is not always the case for the LGBTQ+ community. Therefore, the municipality must extend a lasting helping hand to the community so they can also freely be part of our municipality.

133425.explanation: The VVD wants to optimize or reroute the Staande Mastroute. This will reduce the frequency of bridge openings, improving traffic flow around the bridges.

133426.explanation: A vibrant cultural offering makes Haarlemmermeer attractive, livable, and connected. Culture fosters meeting, expression, and identity. The VVD wants to continue investing in this in the coming years. This also creates space for cultural entrepreneurship.

133369.explanation: We value citizen participation. We want to organize this as effectively and meaningfully as possible so residents can have their voices heard and advise the municipality. A referendum can be one way, but we prefer to focus on substantive discussions.

133370.explanation: We encourage residents to make their neighborhoods more sociable and caring – with help from the municipality, but especially from each other. A neighborhood budget can be used for this.

133371.explanation: Increasing or decreasing the property tax (ozb) is not the goal. It is a tool to ensure a good level of facilities. Only with sound financial policy can we tackle the major challenges of Haarlemmermeer. This means making choices about where we spend money.

133372.explanation: The dog tax is not a target tax. Therefore, it is unnecessary.

133373.explanation: Nature is declining and therefore we must make space for nature restoration, considering all locations, including agriculture.

133374.explanation: The municipality must take control: by making the right spatial choices, supporting residents in sustainability, and enabling new forms of local energy generation and storage. For us, sustainability is not optional; therefore, we support everyone who wants to move forward.

133375.explanation: As a municipality, we adhere to the mandate of the Spread Act for accommodating asylum seekers. Additionally, it may be necessary to relieve places like Ter Apel by temporarily housing more asylum seekers so that people do not have to sleep on grassy areas. We also support bed, bath, and bread arrangements because people should not wander the streets in harsh conditions.

133376.explanation: Solving the housing crisis means building, not pitting groups against each other. Therefore, we ensure enough housing so everyone has a safe home. This applies, for example, to women who are no longer safe at home, economically homeless and roofless people, and status holders.

133378.explanation: Businesses and industrial areas play a crucial role in the climate task. We support entrepreneurs who want to move forward, connect sustainable pioneers, and enforce strictly against pollution. We want to transform industrial areas into future-proof locations.

133379.explanation: Solar panels should ideally be placed on roofs; solar fields (on meadows) and wind turbines should be integrated into the landscape.

133380.explanation: Sports and culture must be accessible to everyone. With our sports pass and culture pass, we ensure that people with low incomes also have access to sports and culture.

133381.explanation: We cherish local heritage. We encourage repurposing valuable, monumental buildings and appreciate the volunteers who work on this. Where possible, we make them more sustainable.

133382.explanation: We want to improve traffic safety around schools and will discuss with them which measures should be taken during student drop-off and pick-up times. Traffic (in)security is a growing problem in Haarlemmermeer.

133383.explanation: We apply the Municipal Act: the mayor can only use camera surveillance when proven necessary. Because it can infringe on privacy and often leads to false security, it is not our preference. We prefer to invest in professionals in the neighborhood and a safely designed public space.

133384.explanation: We advocate for extra control of fatbikes and call for clear national and regional policies with a minimum age of 16 years.

133385.explanation: The use of batons is not supported by laws and regulations. Supervisors advise against it. GroenLinks/PvdA favor other solutions, such as ensuring the police can quickly intervene in public order problems, aggression, or violence.

133386.explanation: We look for instruments to make housing affordable and to keep it that way. Instruments like starter loans, purchase guarantees, and starter purchase schemes will be facilitated in housing projects and made accessible to housing corporations or cooperatives.

133387.explanation: In small communities, it is important that residents can continue living in their own environment. Therefore, we find it important that these people receive priority.

133388.explanation: In 2024, only 14% of delivered homes are affordable; this must change radically. We want at least 80% of all new housing to be affordable for incomes up to 1.5 times the modal income: 40% social rent, 20% middle rent, 20% affordable ownership, and 20% various free-sector owner-occupied and rental homes.

133389.explanation: To solve the housing crisis, we must think in solutions. For mixed functions, densification is the way to build. This means building in existing centers and on their edges. This makes building easier and cheaper because facilities are already nearby.

133390.explanation: The social safety net in the Netherlands and in our municipality is not a favor from the government but a right of our residents. We must not undermine these rights and continue investing in our residents.

133392.explanation: More space for pedestrians and cyclists is important for the livability and safety of our municipality. We investigate locations where 'cars are guests' is possible. Where possible, we design streets as bicycle streets, where cars are guests, and if not possible, as 30 km/h zones.

133393.explanation: We voted against a motion from SRH, which called on the municipal council to no longer involve itself in international conflicts.

133394.explanation: A strong municipal organization is essential to face the challenges of the future. This means the municipality must work efficiently, transparently, and flexibly. It is about an organization that looks ahead and continuously seeks better ways to serve residents.

133395.explanation: We stand for a fair balance: a healthy living environment for residents, drastic sustainability in mobility, and a clear choice for international train connections. We support reducing the number of flight movements as part of national climate goals.

133396.explanation: We advocate for the abolition of night flights because they heavily impact health, quality of life, and climate. This should not result in flights being moved to the edges of the night. We work towards banning night flights from 23:00 to 7:00, so people can start their day well rested.

133397.explanation: Companies must comply with environmental rules and conditions from their permits. Enforcement is not optional: those who pollute cause damage to nature, health, and the living environment.

133398.explanation: We use property tax revenues strategically for maintaining and improving facilities in Haarlemmermeer because our residents have the right to a decent municipality where everyone can participate.

133399.explanation: Information campaigns and education about gender-based violence, relationships, and power dynamics will be expanded, also in schools.

133400.explanation: We focus on wind turbines at locations already designated as search areas. We take livability and the impact on nature and environment into account. We focus on sustainability, where smaller wind turbines are an additional option.

133401.explanation: Young people deserve a good start. We ensure sufficient internship places, traineeships, and guidance towards employment.

133402.explanation: The library is not just a place for books, but primarily for social interaction. We encourage and facilitate its multifunctional use. We will explore whether the library can be made free for all residents.

133403.explanation: We focus on supporting sports clubs. Sports help you get through the day, meet new people, and keep your body healthy. Therefore, it is important that exercise is not only enjoyable but also possible in our municipality. That is why we want to invest in grassroots sports and not in elite sports.

133404.explanation: Events strengthen social cohesion. We stimulate local events in the communities. Additionally, we look at maintaining a balanced number of larger events that put Haarlemmermeer on the map.

133406.explanation: Safety is the foundation for a pleasant and meaningful life in Haarlemmermeer. For GroenLinks-PvdA, this means that residents feel safe on their street, in their neighborhood, and online; that crime and undermining are countered; and that prevention is always a priority.

133407.explanation: Safety is not an isolated theme but intertwined with health, social cohesion, civil rights, and a sustainable living environment. Together with residents, professionals, and partners, we build a Haarlemmermeer where prevention is prioritized over repression and where all voices count.

133408.explanation: Every municipality is different and requires local customization and regulations. At present, affordable housing is not being built in Haarlemmermeer. Therefore, we want local regulations so we can finally start building affordable homes for our residents.

133409.explanation: We focus on building in already urbanized areas. Densification makes housing projects cheaper because infrastructure and facilities are already available.

133410.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA chooses a municipality where everyone can participate and get ahead. We want work to pay off, to fight poverty, and to ensure that no one is excluded. An income to live on means security, dignity, and opportunities for everyone in Haarlemmermeer.

133411.explanation: Young people under 27 who are entitled to social assistance often have to wait 4 weeks for their first payment. This only worsens their problems, such as debts. We will abolish this waiting period.

133412.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants a card for all residents with an income up to 150% of the statutory social minimum. This makes it clearer, easier, and more accessible for residents to see what they are entitled to. We call this the Join In approach.

133413.explanation: Without breakfast, it is harder to learn. That is why the municipality will become a strong partner in the school breakfast program. In our affluent municipality, children should not go to school hungry. This partly determines the opportunities children receive.

133414.explanation: Where parking pressure causes many problems, we look at parking regulation. This can include a permit system, innovation, paid parking (with residents still having the possibility to obtain permits). In this way, we ensure a livable environment.

133415.explanation: More space for pedestrians and cyclists is important for the livability and safety of our municipality. We will investigate locations where 'cars as guests' is possible.

133416.explanation: We plant at least 1,000 new trees net per year; for every tree cut down, at least two will be planted back. Depending on the quality and age of the trees, more trees will be planted.

133417.explanation: Traffic (un)safety is a growing problem in Haarlemmermeer. GroenLinks-PvdA truly wants to change this. Traffic safety will receive higher priority in the municipality, including around schools.

133418.explanation: Parking takes up a lot of space. We have scarce space and must use it sparingly. Parking is a facility offered by businesses. The municipality does not need to pay for this.

133419.explanation: Large parking lots and hardened areas will be transformed where possible with rows of trees, shade structures, and heat-resistant planting.

133420.explanation: We support informal caregivers in time to prevent overburdening. We do this together with organizations that know the neighborhood and recognize the signals.

133421.explanation: When building new neighborhoods, we take different living and care forms into account from the start. Think of intergenerational living, lifetime-proof homes, small-scale housing for people with disabilities or psychological vulnerabilities, and (pre-)informal care homes.

133422.explanation: Health starts with prevention and participation. Therefore, we continue to invest in, stimulate, and facilitate healthy eating and exercise through campaigns and, where possible, partnerships.

133423.explanation: By building on the edges of Park21, we strengthen the green and recreational function and continue construction. Collaboration (co-creation) with current farmers is preferred in the design of Park21. Park21 will be further developed as the large green heart of the municipality.

133424.explanation: The municipality must actively support the LGBTIQ+ community and take a strong stance against all forms of violence, intimidation, or discrimination.

133426.explanation: A healthy living environment also includes space for cultural expression. Therefore, we continue to provide room for Street Art and want to artistically beautify blank walls as much as possible.

133369.explanation: HAP stands for genuine participation. Not just talking, but having influence and thus creating support and sharing responsibility. Therefore, we want an advisory referendum and clear agreements about your role in municipal plans.

133370.explanation: We help residents and businesses to green their environment by providing budgets for local initiatives. Additionally, there is funding available for small initiatives via area management (HAP motion 'Haarlemmermeer Oil').

133371.explanation: Municipal taxes and fees are necessary to implement policy. HAP weighs the necessity and affordability in every choice and puts people at the center. We never ask residents and entrepreneurs for more than strictly necessary and handle public money carefully.

133372.explanation: This tax has just been abolished; as reliable governance, you do not reintroduce it.

133373.explanation: HAP supports the preservation of historic open landscapes, also as a counterbalance to urban densification. If farmers see opportunities to combine their farms with other activities such as recreation, care, innovation, and the energy transition, the municipality must facilitate this. This allows the local agricultural sector to continue working on its future and maintains a balance between city and countryside, and growth and green.

133374.explanation: HAP finds it important that residents who generate sustainable energy together are supported. This can be through knowledge, space, and, where necessary, financial resources. We pay attention to feasibility, equal opportunities, and fair use of public funds.

133375.explanation: Refugees deserve humane shelter and real solutions: Those legally denied asylum in the Netherlands should know this as soon as possible and return to their country of origin. Those granted asylum should receive certainty sooner and not languish for years in reception centers.

133376.explanation: The housing shortage is significant, and many people are looking for affordable housing. As a municipality, we are obliged to meet our legal task for housing status holders, but we believe this can also be done in suitable, non-independent housing and flexible homes.

133378.explanation: Environmental pollution requires clear limits. Companies must adhere to stricter rules. Those who cannot or will not comply do not belong in our municipality.

133379.explanation: If farmers see opportunities to combine their farms with recreation, care, innovation, and the energy transition, the municipality must facilitate this. This allows the local agricultural sector to continue working on its future and maintains a balance between city & countryside, and growth & livability.

133380.explanation: This is established policy. The HDMV minimum scheme ensures that everyone in Haarlemmermeer can remain active and healthy and participate in society. This prevents residents from becoming isolated due to poverty and debt.

133381.explanation: Art and culture make our municipality lively and recognizable. They tell our shared story, from our polder history to today's creative ideas. We want to preserve our unique heritage for future generations.

133382.explanation: Haarlemmermeer must be a safe municipality for everyone: at home, on the street, in shops, and in traffic and public transport. There is a noticeable shortage of 'police presence on the street.' Therefore, more effort is needed. For HAP, the priority lies with a safe living environment, which is broader than just traffic.

133383.explanation: Privacy is important. We use camera surveillance only when truly necessary, appropriate to the location and situation.

133384.explanation: Haarlemmermeer must be a safe municipality for everyone: at home, on the street, in shops, and in traffic and public transport. There is a noticeable shortage of 'police presence on the street.' Therefore, more effort is needed. For HAP, the priority lies with a safe living environment, which is broader than just cyclists.

133385.explanation: As far as HAP is concerned, the law provides the framework for this. If the law allows it, we are not opposed.

133386.explanation: The starter loan helps residents connected to Haarlemmermeer to better access their first home. We fully support this assistance.

133387.explanation: In Haarlemmermeer, priority is given to housing seekers from Haarlemmermeer according to the maximum legally allowed percentages. According to HAP, this should apply to every center in the municipality, including the larger centers like Hoofddorp and Nieuw-Vennep.

133388.explanation: We want more affordable rental and owner-occupied homes, aiming for the national standard of 30% social rental housing in our housing stock. This is our starting point. In new construction, at least 67% of the homes must be affordable (rental or purchase).

133389.explanation: Especially in smaller villages, livability is under pressure. Therefore, expanding a village with housing is sometimes necessary. This can be through adding a layer or an extra street to create a diverse housing supply in the villages as well. Sometimes expansion outside a village boundary is needed to preserve the authentic green character of a village.

133390.explanation: Work is about more than just income; it is also about contributing to society, belonging somewhere, and building valuable social contacts. We therefore believe that those who can work should work, whether paid or as volunteers.

133392.explanation: This is unrealistic and undesirable. 30 km/h zones around schools are excellent; they are effective and feasible. HAP chooses a tailored approach per center when it comes to traffic safety, always in consultation with residents.

133393.explanation: The municipality must be able to speak out about international conflicts in exceptional cases. We do not need to have an opinion on everything, but we let our voice be heard when necessary to protect connection, safety, and justice in our municipality.

133394.explanation: The number of civil servants has a direct relationship with the ambitions of a municipality. Both must be proportional to each other. Reducing the number of civil servants can never be a goal in itself.

133395.explanation: Schiphol must become more sustainable. HAP continues to fight for less nuisance and more responsibility. We advocate limits to growth, better distribution of flights, and stricter environmental standards. Health and livability come first.

133396.explanation: Schiphol must become more sustainable. HAP continues to fight for less nuisance and more responsibility. We advocate limits to growth, better distribution of flights, and stricter environmental standards. Health and livability come first.

133397.explanation: Local SMEs are indispensable as job creators. Besides work, they provide atmosphere and diversity. HAP does not want generic shopping centers but a unique mix of local shops. We must ensure that the rules for SMEs are simple and that contact with the municipality runs smoothly and agreements are honored.

133398.explanation: Municipal taxes and fees are necessary to implement diverse (and not one-sided) policies. HAP weighs necessity and affordability in every decision. We never ask more from residents and entrepreneurs than strictly necessary and handle public funds carefully.

133399.explanation: HAP stands for a respectful Haarlemmermeer where everyone can be themselves, feel accepted and safe, and no one is excluded. By subsidizing organizations such as Discriminatie.nl, we focus on both awareness-raising on these topics and monitoring.

133400.explanation: The province of North Holland is the competent authority for wind farms. This means we do not decide ourselves on granting permits for wind farms in our municipality. We do not want more wind turbines than have been agreed so far.

133401.explanation: HAP encourages internships, learning workplaces, knowledge centers, and experimental education. The connection between education and the labor market is important. Therefore, continuous investment in cooperation between the municipality, local businesses, and education is necessary.

133402.explanation: Access to the library is important for language development, social interaction, and equal opportunities. We want to invest specifically in groups currently falling behind, such as children, youth, and low-income residents.

133403.explanation: HAP believes subsidies are not meant to finance professional sports. Public money should go to sports that get residents moving, strengthen clubs, and lower barriers.

133404.explanation: We especially encourage small-scale local events that connect people of all ages and enable meetings. Large-scale events are valuable in a young municipality but must not harm the livability of residents nearby. Currently, we have enough large-scale events.

133406.explanation: Youth workers, street coaches, enforcement officers, and neighborhood police work closely together to strengthen safety and connection in villages and neighborhoods. Combating crime is part of this. HAP focuses broadly on prevention: against undermining, cybercrime, and other forms of crime.

133407.explanation: Combating crime requires a broad approach. HAP is open to extra resources but only if it is clear where the biggest problems lie and which measures demonstrably work. We choose targeted investments that truly improve safety in our villages and neighborhoods.

133408.explanation: Because there is little land in Haarlemmermeer suitable for housing development, the municipality must retain control. We need a good mix of homes, including affordable ones. Clear rules are needed to make Haarlemmermeer future-proof.

133409.explanation: In existing neighborhoods, space must be used better by adding a layer or an extra street. HAP first sees opportunities in Abbenes, Buitenkaag, and Weteringbrug. All this brings the housing stock more into balance and ensures varied neighborhoods.

133410.explanation: We stand for a municipality where everyone can participate. Prospects of a better future are very important for people with financial worries. That is why HAP focuses on helping residents who are financially struggling. This makes Haarlemmermeer a stronger municipality.

133411.explanation: Young people applying for social assistance benefits must first meet a search period of 4 weeks. This means they must first apply for jobs for 4 weeks before they can claim social assistance. We apply tailored solutions in dire situations.

133412.explanation: Yes, this pass is convenient for people who use minimum income schemes. It prevents them from having to go through complex application processes. As a result, the use of the schemes increases, which is what we want to achieve with our policy.

133413.explanation: We find it important that every child starts the school day with a healthy breakfast. The municipality does not have to finance this; the national government already provides funding for it.

133414.explanation: HAP chooses a mobility approach that fits the scale and character of our villages and neighborhoods. In areas where it is feasible, we stimulate the use of bicycles and public transport. Making parking free everywhere does not contribute to this approach.

133415.explanation: Not every home, school, or facility needs to be accessible by car. HAP chooses a mobility approach that fits the scale and character of our villages and neighborhoods. In areas where it is feasible, we stimulate the use of bicycles and public transport.

133416.explanation: This is a very costly and difficult idea to implement. We do stand for a green environment and livable municipality and follow the principle of one tree out, one tree in.

133417.explanation: That is already happening. We focus on traffic education and awareness because the rise of electric bikes, fatbikes, and microcars has made traffic less safe.

133418.explanation: It is positive when businesses think along about accessibility during shopping evenings. But HAP does not want an automatic compensation scheme. We prefer cooperation, tailored agreements, and solutions that help both entrepreneurs and residents without unnecessary costs to the municipality.

133419.explanation: Greenery in the neighborhood is valuable for health, climate, and livability. HAP does not want choices that increase parking pressure or put entrepreneurs and residents in trouble. We choose a tailored approach: green where possible, preserve where necessary, always in consultation with the neighborhood.

133420.explanation: Informal caregivers play an indispensable role in the daily lives of people with care needs. They deserve recognition and support, as this care demands a lot and is not always easy. Financial compensation is not the solution; good support and respite care are.

133421.explanation: The municipality must facilitate organizations that combine this specific form of care and housing in realizing their plans. That does not automatically mean we must co-finance.

133422.explanation: HAP believes in prevention but not in shifting money without clear results. First, it must be clear which programs work, how they reach residents, and what they deliver. Only then can you responsibly decide to allocate more resources.

133423.explanation: Park21 must be refocused on its core: a green, accessible, and affordable park for all Haarlemmermeer residents. Not a prestige project, but a place where people can exercise, relax, and meet. This requires further development but with clear boundaries.

133424.explanation: HAP stands for a respectful Haarlemmermeer where everyone can be themselves, feel accepted and safe, and no one is excluded. By subsidizing activities, we support awareness and acceptance of this target group.

133425.explanation: Rerouting the Staande Mastroute is not a decision the municipality can make itself. That is up to Rijkswaterstaat. However, HAP believes the municipality must stay actively involved, identify bottlenecks, and advocate for the livability of our villages.

133426.explanation: Art and culture make our municipality lively and recognizable. They tell our shared story, from our polder history to today's creative ideas. Public art is part of this, preferably local and on a modest scale according to HAP.

133369.explanation: D66 advocates involving citizens through citizen councils in major decisions, digital polls, and accessible forms of participation. This gives residents influence over the decisions we make. We especially want to involve people who do not often make their voices heard.

133370.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes that residents who engage in their neighborhood deserve support. By supporting good resident initiatives, the municipality strengthens involvement, social cohesion, and trust, provided plans are feasible and transparent.

133371.explanation: Sometimes investments are needed now to prevent future costs. D66 is willing to slightly increase the property tax if the social benefits of this are clear in advance.

133372.explanation: D66 has always voted against the abolition of the dog tax. Dog owners receive a lot in return for the dog tax, such as well-maintained off-leash areas and waste bins for dog waste. There should be something in return.

133373.explanation: D66, together with farmers, promotes a thriving agricultural sector in Haarlemmermeer, based on natural and sustainable practices and the preservation of biodiversity. In exceptional cases, for example to connect nature areas, agricultural land could be converted into nature reserves.

133374.explanation: D66 wants to accelerate local projects initiated by residents. Therefore, we support local initiatives like energy communities with subsidies and practical assistance.

133375.explanation: Many rejected asylum seekers appeal against their rejection. They are often allowed to wait for the procedure's outcome within the Netherlands, during which they have the right to shelter. Haarlemmermeer must also provide that shelter.

133376.explanation: D66 stands for careful and humane reception and housing of people in need of protection. We apply a fair and transparent housing allocation system for everyone within legal frameworks. D66 strives for a home for everyone, prioritizing vulnerable groups with broad urgency, including status holders.

133378.explanation: D66 believes it is important to balance prosperity and wellbeing. Therefore, D66 wants economic decisions that strengthen health, sustainability, and equality and aims, together with the business community, to realize a green economy that contributes to our climate goals.

133379.explanation: D66 wants a municipality that participates in the fight against climate change. We adhere to the Paris climate agreement. We do this, for example, by embracing sustainable energy. Solar panels on meadows are temporarily allowed, as is currently the case in Zonnecarré.

133380.explanation: D66 is committed to the healthiest generation ever, starting with children. Everyone should be able to participate in sports or join a sports club. For people with a tight budget, we must take an extra step to ensure everyone can join in.

133381.explanation: Haarlemmermeer has few monuments. We must take good care of the monuments we have so that future generations can also enjoy them.

133382.explanation: The contribution of BOAs (community officers) mainly focuses on enhancing safety and livability in our neighborhoods, alongside traffic safety. D66 Haarlemmermeer prioritizes prevention for traffic safety: safe school routes, more 30 km/h zones, and behavioral measures. Additionally, D66 wants an approach against reckless fatbike riding.

133383.explanation: D66 is generally not in favor of extensive camera surveillance. However, we see that in certain places in our municipality nuisance and feelings of insecurity are increasing. Camera surveillance can help prevent problems, and in such cases and locations, D66 supports this monitoring tool.

133384.explanation: D66 wants an approach to combat reckless fatbike riding.

133385.explanation: Under strict conditions, BOAs may be equipped with a baton. This is the case in Haarlemmermeer. If a baton is part of their equipment, it should be usable. However, D66 is not in favor of further arming BOAs.

133386.explanation: Without a starter's loan, buying a house in Haarlemmermeer is not possible for many first-time buyers.

133387.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer wants residents to be able to continue living in their own environment by creating more homes and suitable housing types. At the same time, D66 Haarlemmermeer believes that the allocation of social rental housing must be fair and transparent. Local ties may be taken into account. Urgent housing needs and equal opportunities remain leading.

133388.explanation: For new construction, we aim for 35% social rental, 10% mid-range rent, and 20% affordable owner-occupied homes (mainly for starters). Because home ownership contributes to the financial self-reliance of residents in the long term, we prioritize affordable homeownership over mid-range rent.

133389.explanation: The housing shortage is so severe that new construction beyond the existing village boundaries is necessary. However, D66 also focuses on smart densification by actively tackling vacancies and adding floors to existing buildings and homes.

133390.explanation: The reciprocal obligation is already stated in the Participation Act and is already implemented by the municipality. D66 supports that people with benefits, if able, perform unpaid community work.

133392.explanation: In the town centers, the speed limit is a maximum of 30 kilometers per hour. We are a municipality with many cars, but D66 wants to prioritize livability. Cycling and walking take priority. This requires wide and well-maintained cycle paths, safe school routes, and pleasant walking connections.

133393.explanation: International conflicts can directly affect safety, livability, discrimination, and refugee reception. Therefore, the municipality must sometimes take a stand. For example, against hate, violence, and human rights violations.

133394.explanation: D66 wants to see plans implemented. Currently, Haarlemmermeer has fewer civil servants than comparable municipalities. To maintain the livability of our existing neighborhoods and build enough new homes, we cannot reduce the number of civil servants.

133395.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes that expansion of the number of flights should not lead to more health risks, nuisance, and environmental damage. If further growth of Schiphol exceeds those limits, the municipality must actively oppose it and stand up for residents' interests.

133396.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes health and livability weigh heavily in decisions about Schiphol. Night flights cause serious nuisance and health risks. The municipality must continue to actively strive, within its possibilities, to reduce the number of night flights as much as possible.

133397.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer wants to give entrepreneurs space but believes that sometimes the municipality needs additional rules to protect livability, safety, and sustainability. Regulatory burden must remain limited, but local customization remains necessary.

133398.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes any property tax increase should be aimed at investments with clear social value. This can concern climate and sustainability, but also care, safety, livability, and equal opportunities. The principle applies: fairly distributed and only when genuinely necessary.

133399.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes that equality and combating discrimination require active effort. By investing in awareness and prevention, the municipality works on safety, inclusion, and a society where everyone feels seen and respected.

133400.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer wants to seriously implement the energy transition. To meet climate goals, it may be necessary, carefully and together with residents, to generate additional sustainable energy.

133401.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer wants to invest in good internships for practically trained young people. This gives them work experience, prevents dropout, and strengthens craftsmanship and the regional labor market.

133402.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes libraries are an accessible basic provision. Free access increases equal opportunities, stimulates language and digital skills, and prevents exclusion. Investing in libraries yields social benefits.

133403.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes municipal sports subsidies are intended to make sports and exercise accessible to everyone. Supporting professional sports as a career does not fit here; the focus is on health, prevention, and broad social participation.

133404.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer is open to events that contribute to vibrancy and social encounters but believes they must suit the location and environment.

133406.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer wants to invest in crime prevention by combining prevention, visible enforcement, and neighborhood cooperation. By detecting and addressing problems early, we keep neighborhoods safe and prevent greater damage later.

133407.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer chooses a safe living environment with visible enforcers in the neighborhood. Additional BOAs contribute to safety and approachability, especially when they collaborate with youth work and neighborhood teams to detect and prevent problems early.

133408.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer wants to accelerate housing construction by removing unnecessary rules but maintains local building regulations that ensure quality, livability, and sustainability. Municipal frameworks are needed to build healthy, future-proof neighborhoods and prevent problems afterward.

133409.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer chooses a balanced housing strategy. We build as much as possible inside and near existing villages by smart densification and utilizing vacancies. This keeps villages livable, preserves green landscapes, and locates homes near amenities, work, and public transport.

133410.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer chooses social justice and prevention. Targeted support for people with low incomes strengthens subsistence security and prevents debt, health problems, and more intensive care later.

133411.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes young people applying for social assistance should quickly receive clarity and support to avoid uncertainty. At the same time, we mainly want to motivate and support young people in finding work or education. If waiting times lead to financial problems, customized solutions are always possible.

133412.explanation: D66 supports a card for targeted and automatic support instead of a general card. D66 prefers investing directly in poverty reduction, accessibility of facilities, and automatic arrangements that relieve people. The Haarlemmermeer pass can contribute to this.

133413.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer believes every child has the right to a healthy and opportunity-rich start. Investing in a school breakfast promotes health, better learning outcomes, and equal opportunities, and prevents problems and costs later.

133414.explanation: Making parking free everywhere sounds appealing, but it leads to more congestion, more cars, and less space for greenery and safety. D66 Haarlemmermeer therefore opts for tailored solutions: regulate where necessary and reinvest the proceeds back into the neighborhood.

133415.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer prioritizes cycling and walking, with safe and well-maintained bike paths and safe school routes. This means that cars will no longer always have priority in public spaces.

133416.explanation: D66 wants to invest in greening streets and neighborhoods to prevent heat stress, water nuisance, and health problems. Therefore, we want to double the number of trees in the municipality.

133417.explanation: Traffic safety starts with awareness. Programs for young people help prevent accidents.

133418.explanation: D66 wants lively centers and good accessibility, but chooses collective solutions (public parking, public transport, cycling, well-designed centers), not individual buyouts of parking pressure by entrepreneurs.

133419.explanation: Greenery in the neighborhood is important for health and livability. Replacing parking spaces with greenery can be done, provided it is done carefully and in consultation with residents, with attention to accessibility and alternatives.

133420.explanation: Informal caregivers play an indispensable role. Appreciation and continuing, easily accessible support are important. D66 Haarlemmermeer focuses on combining care with work (flexibility), respite care, and accessible support in the neighborhood.

133421.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer supports investing in sufficient and appropriate care facilities for people with severe mental health problems, but always combined with guidance, distribution, and proper embedding in the neighborhood. Not investing leads to nuisance on the streets and unsafe situations.

133422.explanation: The central ambition of D66 Haarlemmermeer is "The healthiest generation ever." Prevention fits this because health does not start with care but with its prevention; a healthy living environment, mental resilience, early detection, and support in the neighborhood.

133423.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer advocates more green and less concrete, climate-resilient design, and space for recreation close to home. However, D66 wants to continuously critically assess phasing, costs, and social returns.

133424.explanation: D66 stands for equal opportunities and safety for everyone, regardless of sexual orientation or gender identity. We support social initiatives that contribute to inclusion, trust, and prevention because they prevent problems instead of having to fix them afterwards.

133425.explanation: Rerouting the Staande Mastroute is a significant measure affecting multiple municipalities and interests. Such decisions must be made regionally and carefully.

133426.explanation: D66 Haarlemmermeer views public art not as a luxury but as an investment in livability, health, and encounter. We will not stop investing in art but will ensure investments demonstrably contribute to the quality of our neighborhoods and villages.

133369.explanation: Referenda increase and sharpen divisions in society; the CDA does not support this. Referenda reduce complex decisions to a yes or no choice, which does not do justice to the complexity of many proposals.

133370.explanation: We stand for a government that supports where needed and provides space where possible. Think of a helping hand for a neighborhood initiative or making it easier for volunteers to do their work.

133371.explanation: We keep the property tax (OZB) as low as possible. It directly affects the purchasing power of families and households. Therefore, we support an inflation cap on the OZB. The OZB will not increase by more than inflation in the coming period.

133372.explanation: The CDA opposes reintroducing the dog tax. Although we were against its abolition, reinstating it would not demonstrate reliable governance.

133373.explanation: The CDA is not categorically opposed. The CDA supports preserving the polder landscape of Haarlemmermeer. Both nature and agricultural land play an important role here.

133374.explanation: The CDA supports green energy projects. We believe in cooperation with the community so that sustainable initiatives have local support. This transition to a climate-friendly municipality requires everyone's involvement, with the municipality facilitating and supporting.

133375.explanation: The CDA believes every municipality must take responsibility to address the asylum crisis. However, in the case of rejected asylum seekers, we believe the focus must be on their return to the country of origin.

133376.explanation: We do not accommodate more asylum seekers, status holders, and refugees (Ukraine) than we are required to by the dispersal law. We therefore oppose additional municipal regulations such as priority schemes from the municipality. We continue to fulfill our responsibility for refugee reception. We focus on decent accommodation for refugees and displaced persons as far as our responsibility extends.

133378.explanation: The CDA is committed to a livable environment where we jointly care for the future of our planet. From this perspective, the CDA wants to support companies in improving their sustainability. However, we do not want to exclude companies that (still) do not meet these sustainability requirements.

133379.explanation: Ground-mounted solar panels are necessary to enable the energy transition. We adhere to the agreements in the Regional Energy Strategy (RES) and limit the amount of solar parks in Haarlemmermeer to a maximum of 300 hectares.

133380.explanation: We want to keep sports accessible so that every resident - young and old - can feel connected.

133381.explanation: The CDA believes we must care for our environment, heritage, and monuments.

133382.explanation: We want more visible deployment of neighborhood and youth officers and enforcement officers (BOAs) by expanding capacity.

133383.explanation: Camera surveillance can be helpful in tackling crime and identifying perpetrators. In the center of Hoofddorp, camera surveillance should be permanently deployed, and the mayor should have the authority to temporarily implement camera surveillance when necessary.

133384.explanation: We want to improve safety on bike paths. Electric bikes, other electric vehicles, and fatbikes are becoming increasingly popular but sometimes cause unsafe situations or nuisance. We want to increase enforcement here. Safe residential streets have priority.

133385.explanation: The CDA wants better protection for BOAs due to increased aggression. Therefore, we want body cameras to be part of the standard equipment of (green) BOAs, including the option to carry a baton and pepper spray.

133386.explanation: The CDA sees the starter loan as important support for local young adults to buy a home in their own village.

133387.explanation: We give maximum priority to local residents in new construction in small communities (50% for local residents). Additionally, we want to make this possible for specific areas in Nieuw-Vennep and Hoofddorp.

133388.explanation: We focus on mixed neighborhoods: a social mix of social rent, middle rent, and owner-occupied housing. We build at least two-thirds affordable housing with a minimum of 30% social rent. This follows the Housing Management Act (WWRV).

133389.explanation: The CDA supports adding a street or block in every village with maximum priority for local residents (inside or outside the village border). When choosing locations, nature and farmland are relieved as much as possible to preserve the municipality’s polder landscape.

133390.explanation: We advocate careful spending of tax money so that we can invest effectively in poverty reduction and social safety nets. Fighting poverty is done by providing financial support and giving people the opportunity to improve their own situation.

133392.explanation: The CDA supports the current situation where appropriate speeds are determined per location.

133393.explanation: For the CDA, the principle is that the municipal council does not take a position on topics under consideration at the national or international political levels.

133394.explanation: Municipal civil servants are necessary to provide quality services to our residents. Reducing the number of civil servants will lead to longer waiting times for residents.

133395.explanation: A reliable government is not only decisive but also respects the applicable laws and regulations. Courts have clearly indicated in earlier rulings that there are limits to the growth of Schiphol and that the health of residents deserves sufficient protection.

133396.explanation: The CDA aims to further reduce the number of disturbing night flights.

133397.explanation: The CDA believes there should be a good business climate for (starting) entrepreneurs. This is also beneficial for expanding employment. The municipal government, from the CDA’s perspective, wants to remove barriers and unnecessary regulations.

133398.explanation: We keep the property tax (OZB) as low as possible. It directly affects the purchasing power of families and households. Therefore, we support an inflation cap on the OZB. The OZB will not increase by more than inflation in the coming period.

133399.explanation: We focus on a robust approach to domestic violence, child abuse, and discrimination. With easily accessible reporting points for victims, early detection of home situations, and campaigns for inclusion, we strengthen safety.

133400.explanation: For the realization of new wind turbines in Haarlemmermeer South, we adhere to the established rules and the current search area. We do not support increasing the number or locating wind turbines in other parts of Haarlemmermeer.

133401.explanation: We encourage internships and work-learning trajectories in which students gain practical experience and employers can retain young talent. Additionally, we support re- and further training so workers can adapt to changing economic needs.

133402.explanation: Solidarity and justice form an important foundation for financial choices for the CDA. We find it fair to ask people to contribute to the use of common facilities such as libraries. Where necessary, we provide appropriate financial support through our generous poverty policy. After all, everyone must have access to basic provisions.

133403.explanation: For the CDA, sport and physical activity are priorities. Sport brings people together, promotes physical and mental health, and contributes to community spirit. The CDA advocates for accessible and affordable sports facilities so sports are available to all ages and backgrounds. Subsidizing elite sports does not fit our local responsibilities and we see that as the task of national sports federations and the central government through NOC-NSF.

133404.explanation: Instead of focusing on large events that attract many people from outside the polder to Haarlemmermeer, the CDA wants to focus on local events that improve social cohesion within the community.

133406.explanation: We fully prioritize prevention in tackling (youth) crime. This includes more space for youth workers, mentors, and other initiatives to keep young people on the right path and maintain connections with one another.

133407.explanation: We want more visible deployment of neighborhood and youth officers and enforcement officers (BOAs) by expanding capacity.

133408.explanation: Although CDA Haarlemmermeer strongly supports abolishing unnecessary rules, certain rules regarding, for example, accessibility and design remain necessary to maintain the quality of the living environment valued by residents (sufficient green space, room for movement, and good facilities). People enjoy living in Haarlemmermeer because of the quality of the physical living environment. That quality can only be ensured by retaining essential municipal regulations.

133409.explanation: The CDA believes that new construction should take place both on the outskirts and within the cores of the villages.

133410.explanation: We advocate careful spending of tax money so that we can effectively invest in poverty reduction, social safety nets, and provisions for vulnerable groups. The CDA wants to maintain the existing budget for minimum income policy.

133411.explanation: The CDA supports procedures that are as short as possible, but it is unrealistic to make a careful decision within two weeks. Moreover, we believe young people should always be encouraged first to follow (new) education or find employment.

133412.explanation: The pass was abolished because it was rarely used. The CDA wants our generous minimum income policy to be more accessible to the most vulnerable residents but does not believe reintroducing the Haarlemmermeer pass is the solution.

133413.explanation: Solidarity and justice form an important foundation for financial choices for the CDA. We stand for a government that supports where necessary and provides space where possible. Responsibility for breakfast lies primarily with parents. Where necessary, we provide appropriate financial support through our generous poverty policy.

133414.explanation: The CDA wants enough new parking spaces to be created during new construction projects. Additionally, the CDA is against further expansion of paid parking. However, there are places in the municipality where paid parking remains necessary to prevent parking nuisance.

133415.explanation: Mobility and infrastructure are essential for a well-functioning society. The CDA advocates for safe, accessible, and sustainable transport options for all residents. Bicycle and pedestrian paths, public transport, and safe roads are important for livability and accessibility.

133416.explanation: The CDA wants to maintain the current number of trees and create more green spaces where possible.

133417.explanation: To counter the trend of increasing traffic casualties, improving traffic safety is a priority. Prevention is an important part of this. We involve residents in plans to make their neighborhood safer in terms of traffic (school streets and multi-purpose use of squares).

133418.explanation: The municipality must ensure that there are enough parking spaces. The CDA does not want to create a separate scheme to privatize the supply of parking spaces.

133419.explanation: The CDA believes that replacing parking spaces for new construction or green areas should only happen with neighborhood support, but we are not opposed to it in principle.

133420.explanation: The CDA stands for a responsible society where people look after one another and care for each other. To temporarily relieve informal caregivers, we advocate for more support. This can be done by funding respite care, daytime activities, and support (information and advice). Providing monetary compensation is, in our view, a task for the national government.

133421.explanation: More and more children grow up in families where parents struggle with trauma, stress, poverty, or mental health problems. We want to prevent recurrence of violence by focusing on family-centered support where youth and adult care collaborate with informal networks, neighborhood police officers, and schools.

133422.explanation: Prevention is an important focus for the CDA to address health problems early and thus relieve pressure on healthcare. Prevention means more to the CDA than just avoiding illness. Therefore, we want to invest in prevention programs.

133423.explanation: The CDA stands for agriculture that is future-proof, with attention to nature and the environment. We see farmers as partners in caring for our landscape and food supply. Therefore, we support giving local farmers an important role in managing Park21.

133424.explanation: We focus on a robust approach to domestic violence, child abuse, and discrimination. With easily accessible reporting points for victims, early detection of home situations, and campaigns for inclusion, we strengthen safety.

133425.explanation: Although the Standing Mast Route can significantly disrupt road traffic flow in Haarlemmermeer during the summer months, the municipality cannot enforce a detour because the provinces of North Holland and South Holland are responsible for this.

133426.explanation: The CDA sees art, culture, and religion as sources of inspiration and strength, especially in times of uncertainty. We want to make culture accessible so that every resident-young and old-can feel connected to our history and pass it on to future generations.

133369.explanation: Forza! is the initiator of the referendum ordinance in Haarlemmermeer and is also an outspoken supporter of further developing the current advisory referendum into a binding referendum. Forza! believes residents should not only be heard but also have real influence on important municipal decisions.

133370.explanation: From our election program: "Forza! wants to do better by making the most of the opportunities the law offers us in tenders and by supporting residents who want to take care of green maintenance themselves with money and resources."

133371.explanation: Forza! supports reducing the property tax (OZB) because residents and entrepreneurs automatically pay more each year due to rising WOZ values without better services. The municipality should first save, set priorities, and limit itself to core tasks instead of increasing the burden.

133372.explanation: Forza! has fought for years to abolish this unfair tax. It was a Forza! motion in 2022 that ended the dog tax. As far as we're concerned, it will never be reintroduced. Dog owners paid for keeping a pet while no specific service was provided in return.

133373.explanation: We are not against more green spaces and recreation, but believe PARK21 is too expensive and agricultural land can be better utilized. The municipality must strictly control astronomical costs and execution and cooperate with farmers and residents.

133374.explanation: Residents who want to jointly generate sustainable energy should, depending on the plans, receive support in the form of sharing knowledge and help with applying for subsidies. Forza! opposes wind turbines or large-scale solar parks on fertile land.

133375.explanation: Forza! believes we should not accommodate asylum seekers at all, especially not rejected asylum seekers. Rejected asylum seekers must return to their country of origin.

133376.explanation: Forza! believes everyone living in the Netherlands should be treated fairly in the allocation of social rental housing. It is unfair that status holders receive priority while Dutch people wait for years. We advocate for equal rules for everyone, with attention to urgency in special situations, so no one is unnecessarily disadvantaged.

133378.explanation: All companies that comply with the law and add value (meaning employment) to our municipality are welcome.

133379.explanation: Forza! prefers that all roofs are covered with solar panels first before fertile agricultural land is used and sacrificed. This was our reason to vote against the solar field.

133380.explanation: Forza! wants to support families with an income up to 120% of the minimum with the so-called Haarlemmermeer Pass, which allows free or discounted use of all sports facilities in the municipality.

133381.explanation: Forza! believes cultural heritage and monuments are an important part of our history and identity. Cuts to monument care would endanger these valuable buildings and traditions. Forza! advocates for the preservation and protection of our heritage so future generations can also enjoy and learn from our culture and history.

133382.explanation: Forza! values traffic safety and sees enforcement as necessary to address dangerous behavior. More enforcement officers are acceptable, provided they are deployed practically where problems exist and not for symbolic policy or unnecessary checks. Safety must visibly improve.

133383.explanation: Forza! supports measures that improve the safety of residents and entrepreneurs, such as camera surveillance in shopping areas. However, this must be effective, proportionate, and conducted with respect for privacy so it is not unnecessarily burdensome or expensive.

133384.explanation: Anyone who behaves rudely in traffic should be confronted about it.

133385.explanation: The monopoly on violence lies with the police and must remain so. In debates, we have always opposed arming enforcement officers (BOAs).

133386.explanation: Forza! wants to help first-time buyers by building more affordable homes and removing obstructive rules. The starter loan drives up prices and does not address the housing shortage. The municipality must focus on structural solutions instead of temporary financial support.

133387.explanation: Totally agree. Livability and community life are indispensable. Our election program is filled with prioritizing our own residents.

133388.explanation: The most important thing is that we finally start building on a large scale. Forza! wants our municipality to adhere to national standards without adding extra requirements. The current norm is that 50% must be affordable.

133389.explanation: Forza! has never supported densification. We consider new construction important and want to use all possibilities, including outside existing village boundaries, to realize affordable housing.

133390.explanation: Forza! has always advocated for a reciprocal contribution. We believe that people receiving social assistance can contribute to society as long as the work is meaningful and does not discourage them from seeking independent employment.

133392.explanation: Forza! does not support a uniform 30 km/h speed limit in all towns. We value traffic safety but limit speed reductions to villages and neighborhoods with a lot of cut-through traffic and dangerous situations. This keeps traffic flowing smoothly where possible while protecting safety where truly needed.

133393.explanation: Forza! believes the municipality should focus on local matters and not comment on international conflicts.

133394.explanation: Agreed. The municipality should play a much more facilitating and policy-setting role and interfere less with execution. Forza! supports reducing government size.

133395.explanation: Forza! is not automatically against Schiphol or growth in flight numbers. The municipality must oppose expansion if it leads to more nuisance, health risks, or deterioration of livability. Growth is only negotiable within clear, strict limits and with residents at the table.

133396.explanation: We do not want to ban, but we aim to reduce the number of night flights. By 2030, the number of night flights should be at least 20% lower than in 2023.

133397.explanation: The municipality should never add more rules on top of national laws.

133398.explanation: Forza! believes property tax (OZB) should be lowered, regardless of specific purposes like climate or sustainability. Residents should not pay unnecessarily more.

133399.explanation: You can't turn on the TV without hearing about equality and alleged discrimination. Forza! thinks that has been enough.

133400.explanation: Forza! says no to more wind turbines in our polder. Our landscape, peace, and resident support must not be sacrificed for unrealistic energy goals. Forza! opposes wind turbines even in areas designated by the council as search zones.

133401.explanation: Forza! believes young people should have enough internship places, but these should come from companies establishing themselves in the municipality, not direct municipal spending. The municipality must ensure good business policies so companies offer internships.

133402.explanation: Forza! values libraries and believes they should be accessible, but is critical of unnecessary costs. We want residents to be able to use libraries, but free access for everyone is not automatically necessary; the municipality must manage funds efficiently and effectively. Additionally, libraries are already free for youth up to 27. With the Haarlemmermeer Pass we want to introduce, it could be free for people with modest incomes.

133403.explanation: Top-level and professional sports should not receive subsidies. They do not contribute to the core municipal sports policy: enabling as many residents as possible to sport and exercise healthily, safely, and nearby. Municipal sports funds should support grassroots sports and sports that foster meeting, participation, and inclusion.

133404.explanation: Forza! believes events can contribute to the economy and livability but only if well-organized and without causing nuisance or high costs for residents. There should be a clear maximum on large events, strict rules on light, noise, and end times, half the complaints about events, and not too many events in Haarlemmerliede and Spaarndam.

133406.explanation: Forza! considers safety a top priority and wants the municipality to allocate sufficient resources to actively combat crime and protect residents.

133407.explanation: Forza! believes more municipal enforcement officers are needed. Currently, too many reports go unaddressed due to a lack of officers. It is high time the municipality fills vacancies and expands capacity so safety is properly enforced.

133408.explanation: Agreed. No rules = building everywhere and too many rules = no building. We are already too close to a situation where building is hardly or not possible.

133409.explanation: New housing must be built where possible. At village edges to keep them livable and inside built-up areas where there is space. We simply must build.

133410.explanation: Forza! wants to maintain support for residents with low incomes but make it simpler and more effective, among other things by reintroducing the Haarlemmermeer Pass. The goal is not less support but better support that helps people participate and become more independent. Forza! does not want to keep the current minimum schemes but reform them (including reintroduction of the Haarlemmermeer Pass).

133411.explanation: Forza! believes young people entitled to social assistance should be helped quickly to avoid financial problems, keeping in mind personal responsibility.

133412.explanation: Forza! believes everyone should be able to participate in sports, culture, and activities. The municipality must reintroduce the Haarlemmermeer Pass and use it smartly and purposefully so that people with low incomes truly get opportunities and are not excluded.

133413.explanation: No child should sit in class without breakfast, but that responsibility really lies with the parents.

133414.explanation: Forza! supports accessible parking for residents and entrepreneurs but not free parking everywhere. Free parking attracts parking pressure from neighboring cities like Amsterdam and risks turning our centers into ‘the parking lot of Schiphol’ or Amsterdam transfer locations, causing nuisance and costs for our residents. Our focus is on clear, fair, and affordable parking options.

133415.explanation: Haarlemmermeer is well accessible by bike. It’s not the number of bike paths but safety and maintenance condition that matter. Let’s focus on that first.

133416.explanation: Planting trees is the best way to provide cooling and capture CO2.

133417.explanation: Forza! wants the municipality to implement active programs for youth to increase traffic safety. Young people must be able to travel safely, handle traffic consciously, and prevent accidents!

133418.explanation: Forza! believes companies and retailers can make their own agreements about reimbursements; the municipality does not need to spend money on this.

133419.explanation: In all village centers of Haarlemmermeer, using a car is essential. Replacing parking spaces with greenery as an objective in itself seems like a bad idea to us.

133420.explanation: We support enabling respite care (the temporary takeover of (part of) care for a loved one so the informal caregiver can rest and recharge to sustain care long-term) but not paying informal caregivers.

133421.explanation: There are sufficient locations that the municipality funds fulfilling its legal obligation regarding WMO care. For structural issues covered by the Long-Term Care Act (WLZ), health insurers are responsible. However, we must continue to monitor the quality of existing care, maintain it, and safeguard it.

133422.explanation: Agreed, especially to reduce youth care costs.

133423.explanation: Forza! believes PARK21 threatens to become an endless money pit. Instead of wasting billions, the municipality should allow nature to develop as it should, in a smart and natural way, without residents footing the huge bills.

133424.explanation: Our lead candidate, part of those acronyms, says such a community does not exist. Haarlemmermeer receives a (relatively modest) annual contribution from the Ministry of Education, Culture and Science to execute local emancipation policy, which is more than sufficient. Municipal subsidies must be targeted and efficient.
